title: The Surprising Origin of Human Eyes: From a One-Eyed Ancestor
summary: New research from Lund University and the University of Sussex reveals that all vertebrates, including humans, share a common ancestor that had a single eye on top of its head. This creature, which lived about 600 million years ago, was a small, worm-like animal that filtered plankton from seawater. Earlier in its evolution, it likely had two eyes, but as it adapted to a stationary lifestyle, those eyes disappeared. However, it retained a cluster of light-sensitive cells in the center of its head, which eventually formed a simple median eye that could detect light and darkness. When the ancestor became more active and returned to a swimming lifestyle, parts of this median eye evolved into the paired eyes we have today. This explains why vertebrate eyes are built differently from those of insects and squid. In vertebrates, the retina develops from the brain, while in other animals, eyes originate from skin tissues. The findings challenge previous understanding of eye evolution and highlight the complex path that led to modern vision.

background_read: Evolution is the process by which living things change over generations. Sometimes, animals lose features they no longer need, like the paired eyes in this ancestor. Later, they may develop new features from existing structures. The median eye is an example of a structure that changed function over time. The pineal gland in our brain is a leftover from this ancient eye.
Article_Structure: The article presents a surprising discovery about vertebrate eye evolution. Main points: a worm-like ancestor with a single eye gave rise to all vertebrates. Purpose: to explain why vertebrate eyes are different from other animals' eyes. Evidence: based on research from Lund University and University of Sussex, analyzing evolutionary history. Author credibility: Dan-E Nilsson is a professor emeritus in sensory biology, adding authority. Methodology: likely involved comparative anatomy and phylogenetic analysis. The evidence is strong but relies on indirect fossil and genetic data.

perspectives.description: This discovery changes the understanding of eye evolution and highlights the role of lifestyle changes.
The difference in eye development between vertebrates and invertebrates is now explained by a common one-eyed ancestor.
